llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: The study uses a difference-in-differences design comparing GitHub Pro users (who have access to Copilot X) to non-Pro users (control). Table 6, Column 2 reports Code Churn % as a dependent variable with Pro × PostAnnouncement and Pro × PostLaunch interaction terms. The coefficients represent the treatment effect on code churn percentage. While the paper states 'no statistically significant change in the post-announcement period' and 'modestly positive after launch,' Table 6 Column 2 provides the actual regression coefficients: Pro × PostAnnouncement = -0.00 (SE 0.02, p>0.05) and Pro × PostLaunch = 0.01 (SE 0.01, p>0.05). These are regression coefficients from a panel regression with user and week fixed effects. The sample includes N=2476 user-week observations from 229 users (per Table 6 footer). This is an observational quasi-experimental design, not a randomized trial, but it does compare a human-only baseline (non-Pro users without CX) to a human+AI condition (Pro users with CX access).
llm_extracted.notes: Table 6 Column 2 reports regression coefficients for Code Churn % outcome. Pro × PostAnnouncement coefficient = -0.00 (SE 0.02), Pro × PostLaunch coefficient = 0.01 (SE 0.01). Standard errors are clustered at user level. The paper states these effects are not statistically significant (p>0.05 implied but exact p-values not reported). N=2476 observations from 229 users. This is a panel regression with user and week fixed effects, not a simple two-group comparison, so means and SDs are not reported. The regression coefficients represent percentage-point changes in code churn. Without means, SDs, or a t-statistic, we cannot compute Hedges' g. The study design is observational (Pro vs non-Pro users) rather than a controlled experiment. Code churn is a 'smaller is better' metric (lower churn = better code stability), so direction_convention is set accordingly. The sample size of 229 users is taken from the '# Users' row in Table 6.
source_quotes: Table 6, Column 2: Pro × PostAnnouncement = -0.00 (0.02), Pro × PostLaunch = 0.01 (0.01)
Code Churn % shows no statistically significant change in the post-announcement period, and the estimated effect becomes modestly positive after launch.
Code Churn % [is the] Percentage of code added in the week that is modified or removed within the subsequent 2 weeks
Standard errors are clustered at the user level. * p < 0.1, ** p < 0.05, *** p < 0.01
